topics.title: Study of responses of P, PI and PID controllers on Level/Flow/Thermal/Pressure processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL507 - PROCESS CONTROL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of responses of P, PI and PID controllers on Level/Flow/Thermal/Pressure processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In process control engineering, a **controller** is a feedback mechanism that continuously compares the **Process Variable (PV)** with the desired **Set Point (SP)** and generates a corrective **Manipulated Variable (MV)** to drive the process error to zero. The three fundamental controller modes studied in PCAEL507 are:

### P Controller (Proportional)
A controller whose output is directly proportional to the error signal $e(t) = SP - PV$. The mathematical model is:
$$m(t) = m_0 + K_c \, e(t)$$
where $K_c$ is the **proportional gain** (dimensionless or %/%), and $m_0$ is the controller bias.

### PI Controller (Proportional + Integral)
Augments the P-mode with an integral term that accumulates past errors, eliminating steady-state offset:
$$m(t) = m_0 + K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau \right]$$
where $\tau_I$ is the **integral time** (also called *reset time*) in **minutes** or **seconds/repeat**.

### PID Controller (Proportional + Integral + Derivative)
Adds a derivative term that anticipates future error based on its current rate of change:
$$m(t) = m_0 + K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$
where $\tau_D$ is the **derivative time** (also called *rate time*) in **minutes** or **seconds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the laboratory, these three controllers are tested on **four canonical process types** — **Level** (integrating), **Flow** (fast first-order), **Thermal** (first-order plus dead time), and **Pressure** (first-order with or without dead time). Each process has a *characteristic* response to controller tuning.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **driving a car** and your goal is to maintain a constant speed of **60 km/h** (this is the **Set Point**). Your eyes observe the speedometer (**PV**), and your foot controls the accelerator (**MV**).

- **P Controller (Proportional only):** The harder the speed deviates from 60 km/h, the more you press the accelerator. But you always leave a "small gap" because pressing proportionally means you'll never quite push enough at low errors — this leaves a permanent **offset** (the car settles at 58 km/h instead of 60 km/h).

- **PI Controller (Add Integral):** You keep nudging the accelerator over time as long as ANY error remains, no matter how tiny. Eventually, you eliminate the 2 km/h gap completely — **no offset**, but you may oscillate a bit before settling.

- **PID Controller (Add Derivative):** You see a sharp hill ahead, so you preemptively press the brake slightly *before* the speed actually drops — that's the **derivative** term acting on the rate of change. This makes the ride smoother with **less overshoot**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Process Analogy Map for PCAEL507:**
> - **Level** ↔ A water tank with a drain — naturally self-balancing if inflow = outflow.
> - **Flow** ↔ Water in a pipe — reacts almost instantly to a valve change.
> - **Thermal** ↔ Heating a beaker of water — slow, with a noticeable *time delay* between heater ON and temperature rise.
> - **Pressure** ↔ Pressurizing a vessel — fast but with some inertia and possible transport delay.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold)

The following standard metrics are used in lab measurement and analysis:

- **Process Gain ($K_p$):** Change in PV per unit change in MV (units: %/%).
- **Time Constant ($\tau_p$):** Time for PV to reach **63.2%** of its final value for a step input (units: **seconds**).
- **Dead Time / Transport Delay ($L$ or $T_d$):** Pure time delay between MV change and PV response (units: **seconds**).
- **Steady-State Error (Offset):** $e_{ss} = SP - PV_\infty$ (units: **%** of span).
- **Rise Time ($t_r$):** Time to go from 10% to 90% of final value.
- **Settling Time ($t_s$):** Time to enter and stay within ±2% (or ±5%) of final value.
- **Overshoot ($M_p$):** Maximum peak above the final value, expressed as a percentage.
- **Integral of Absolute Error (IAE):** $\int_0^\infty \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$ — used as a tuning performance index.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Closed-Loop Control Architecture

Every process control loop in PCAEL507 follows a canonical negative-feedback structure. The **error detector** compares SP and PV, the **controller** ($G_c$) computes the corrective action, the **final control element** (valve/heater) implements it, and the **process** ($G_p$) responds. The **sensor/transmitter** ($H$) feeds the PV back.

### 2.1.1 Standard Closed-Loop Transfer Function

For unity feedback ($H(s)=1$), the closed-loop transfer function relating PV to SP is:
$$T(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{G_c(s) G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)}$$

The error transfer function is:
$$\frac{E(s)}{R(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)}$$

The **characteristic equation** governing stability and dynamic response is:
$$1 + G_c(s) G_p(s) = 0$$

---

## 2.2 Controller Transfer Functions in the Laplace Domain

| Controller | Time Domain $m(t)$ | Transfer Function $G_c(s)$ |
|---|---|---|
| **P** | $K_c \, e(t)$ | $K_c$ |
| **PI** | $K_c\!\left[e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\!\int e\,dt\right]$ | $K_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right) = \dfrac{K_c(\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s}$ |
| **PD** | $K_c\!\left[e(t) + \tau_D \dfrac{de}{dt}\right]$ | $K_c(1 + \tau_D s)$ |
| **PID** | $K_c\!\left[e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\!\int e\,dt + \tau_D \dfrac{de}{dt}\right]$ | $K_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s\right) = \dfrac{K_c(\tau_I \tau_D s^2 + \tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The P-mode reduces rise time but **introduces offset**. The I-mode **eliminates offset** but may cause oscillation. The D-mode **improves stability** and reduces overshoot, but amplifies measurement noise.

---

## 2.3 Canonical Process Models Used in PCAEL507

| Process | Typical Transfer Function $G_p(s)$ | Characteristic |
|---|---|---|
| **Level** (tank with outflow) | $\dfrac{K_p}{s}$ or $\dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$ (self-regulating) | **Integrating** or first-order; *no offset* with P |
| **Flow** (pipe + valve) | $\dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$ with small $\tau_p$ | **Fast first-order**; almost no dead time |
| **Thermal** (heater + thermocouple) | $\dfrac{K_p \, e^{-Ls}}{\tau_p s + 1}$ | **First-order + dead time (FOPDT)** |
| **Pressure** (vessel) | $\dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$ or $\dfrac{K_p \, e^{-Ls}}{\tau_p s + 1}$ | First-order, sometimes with delay |

The general **First-Order Plus Dead Time (FOPDT)** model used in KTU lab experiments is:
$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p \, e^{-L s}}{\tau_p s + 1}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below consolidates every formula you must memorize for PCAEL507 Module Experiment 2. Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ for absolute value to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G_c^P(s) = K_c$ | P controller |
| 2 | $G_c^{PI}(s) = \dfrac{K_c(\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s}$ | PI controller |
| 3 | $G_c^{PID}(s) = \dfrac{K_c(\tau_I \tau_D s^2 + \tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s}$ | PID controller |
| 4 | $T(s) = \dfrac{G_c G_p}{1 + G_c G_p}$ | Closed-loop TF (unity feedback) |
| 5 | $E(s) = \dfrac{R(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)}$ | Error TF |
| 6 | $e_{ss}^{P} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_c K_p}$ (first-order process) | Steady-state error with P-only |
| 7 | $e_{ss}^{PI} = 0$ for step input | Integral removes offset |
| 8 | $\tau_{cl} = \dfrac{\tau_p}{1 + K_c K_p}$ | Closed-loop time constant with P |
| 9 | $K_{cl} = \dfrac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}$ | Closed-loop gain with P |
| 10 | $M_p = e^{-(\zeta \pi)/\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} \times 100\%$ | % Overshoot (2nd order) |
| 11 | $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ (2% criterion) | Settling time |
| 12 | $t_p = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}$ | Peak time |
| 13 | $K_c^{max} = \dfrac{\tau_p}{K_p L}$ (Z-N, for FOPDT) | Ultimate P-gain (open-loop) |
| 14 | $K_u = \dfrac{1}{K_p} \cdot \dfrac{\tau_p}{L}$ | Ultimate gain estimate |
| 15 | $\text{IAE} = \int_0^\infty \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$ | Performance index |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding P, PI, PID responses is foundational to:
- **Chemical plants:** Maintaining reactor temperature, distillation column reflux ratio, and tank levels.
- **HVAC systems:** Room temperature and humidity control.
- **Power plants:** Boiler drum level, steam pressure, and turbine speed.
- **Oil & gas:** Flow regulation in pipelines, compressor surge control.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Batch reactor pH and temperature control.

> [!TIP]
> In the **KTU lab**, students are expected to (i) identify the process type from an open-loop step test, (ii) estimate $K_p$, $\tau_p$, $L$, (iii) tune $K_c$, $\tau_I$, $\tau_D$ using either trial-and-error or Ziegler–Nichols, and (iv) record $t_r$, $t_s$, $M_p$, and $e_{ss}$ for **each controller–process combination**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate:** No steps are skipped. Each algebraic transition is shown explicitly with an explanatory line.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Closed-Loop Response of a First-Order Process with P Controller

### Given
- Process: $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$ (first-order, e.g., flow or pressure)
- Controller: $G_c(s) = K_c$ (P-only)
- Feedback: Unity ($H(s) = 1$)

### Step 1 — Form the forward path product
$$G_c(s) G_p(s) = K_c \cdot \frac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1} = \frac{K_c K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$$

### Step 2 — Write the closed-loop transfer function
By definition: $T(s) = \dfrac{G_c G_p}{1 + G_c G_p}$

Substitute:
$$T(s) = \frac{\dfrac{K_c K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}}{1 + \dfrac{K_c K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}}$$

### Step 3 — Combine into a single rational function
Multiply numerator and denominator by $(\tau_p s + 1)$:
$$T(s) = \frac{K_c K_p}{(\tau_p s + 1) + K_c K_p}$$

### Step 4 — Simplify the denominator
$$(\tau_p s + 1) + K_c K_p = \tau_p s + (1 + K_c K_p)$$

### Step 5 — Final closed-loop transfer function
$$\boxed{T(s) = \frac{K_c K_p}{\tau_p s + (1 + K_c K_p)} = \frac{\dfrac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}}{\dfrac{\tau_p}{1 + K_c K_p}\,s + 1}}$$

### Step 6 — Identify closed-loop parameters
Comparing with the standard first-order form $\dfrac{K_{cl}}{\tau_{cl} s + 1}$:
$$K_{cl} = \frac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}, \qquad \tau_{cl} = \frac{\tau_p}{1 + K_c K_p}$$

### Step 7 — Compute steady-state response to unit step $R(s) = 1/s$
Apply the **Final Value Theorem**:
$$y(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \, T(s) \cdot \frac{1}{s} = T(0) = \frac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}$$

### Step 8 — Compute steady-state error
$$e_{ss} = 1 - y(\infty) = 1 - \frac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p} = \frac{1}{1 + K_c K_p}$$

### Step 9 — Interpretation
- $K_c$ is **larger** → $e_{ss}$ is **smaller**, but never zero.
- Increasing $K_c$ indefinitely drives $\tau_{cl} \to 0$ but risks instability if higher-order dynamics exist.
- **Conclusion:** P-only control of a first-order non-integrating process **always exhibits offset**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Closed-Loop Response with PI Controller (Eliminates Offset)

### Given
- Process: $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$
- PI Controller: $G_c(s) = \dfrac{K_c(\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s}$
- Unity feedback.

### Step 1 — Form the open-loop transfer function $L(s) = G_c G_p$
$$L(s) = \frac{K_c(\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s} \cdot \frac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1} = \frac{K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s (\tau_p s + 1)}$$

### Step 2 — Closed-loop TF
$$T(s) = \frac{L(s)}{1 + L(s)} = \frac{K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s (\tau_p s + 1) + K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1)}$$

### Step 3 — Expand the denominator
First, expand the term $\tau_I s(\tau_p s + 1)$:
$$\tau_I s(\tau_p s + 1) = \tau_I \tau_p s^2 + \tau_I s$$

Next, expand $K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1)$:
$$K_c K_p \tau_I s + K_c K_p$$

Add them:
$$\tau_I \tau_p s^2 + \tau_I s + K_c K_p \tau_I s + K_c K_p = \tau_I \tau_p s^2 + \tau_I(1 + K_c K_p)s + K_c K_p$$

### Step 4 — Standard 2nd-order form
$$T(s) = \frac{K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I \tau_p s^2 + \tau_I(1 + K_c K_p) s + K_c K_p}$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $\tau_I \tau_p$:
$$T(s) = \frac{\dfrac{K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I \tau_p}}{s^2 + \dfrac{(1 + K_c K_p)}{\tau_p} s + \dfrac{K_c K_p}{\tau_I \tau_p}}$$

### Step 5 — Identify natural frequency and damping ratio
Comparing with $s^2 + 2\zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^2$:
$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{K_c K_p}{\tau_I \tau_p}, \qquad 2 \zeta \omega_n = \frac{1 + K_c K_p}{\tau_p}$$

Therefore:
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K_c K_p}{\tau_I \tau_p}}, \qquad \zeta = \frac{1 + K_c K_p}{2 \sqrt{K_c K_p \tau_I / \tau_p}}$$

### Step 6 — Steady-state error
Apply Final Value Theorem with $R(s) = 1/s$:
$$y(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot T(s) \cdot \frac{1}{s} = T(0) = \frac{K_c K_p (0 + 1)}{0 + 0 + K_c K_p} = 1$$

Therefore $e_{ss} = 1 - 1 = 0$. **Offset is eliminated** because the integrator in $G_c$ provides infinite DC gain.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Level Process (Integrating) with P Controller

### Given
- Tank level process: $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{s}$ (pure integrator)
- P Controller: $G_c(s) = K_c$

### Step 1 — Open-loop
$$L(s) = \frac{K_c K_p}{s}$$

### Step 2 — Closed-loop
$$T(s) = \frac{K_c K_p / s}{1 + K_c K_p / s} = \frac{K_c K_p}{s + K_c K_p}$$

### Step 3 — Standard first-order form
$$T(s) = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{K_c K_p}\,s + 1}$$

### Step 4 — Time-domain unit-step response
$$y(t) = 1 - e^{-K_c K_p t}$$

### Step 5 — Steady-state
$$y(\infty) = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; e_{ss} = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for PCAEL507:** Because the **level process itself is an integrator**, even a **P-only controller drives the offset to zero** — no integral action is needed for setpoint tracking. This is a *unique* property of integrating processes.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: PID on a Thermal (FOPDT) Process

### Given
- $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p e^{-Ls}}{\tau_p s + 1}$
- $G_c(s) = \dfrac{K_c(\tau_I \tau_D s^2 + \tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s}$

### Step 1 — Characteristic equation (ignoring $e^{-Ls}$ for analysis)
$$1 + G_c G_p = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \tau_I s(\tau_p s + 1) + K_c K_p(\tau_I \tau_D s^2 + \tau_I s + 1) = 0$$

### Step 2 — Expand
$$\tau_I \tau_p s^2 + \tau_I s + K_c K_p \tau_I \tau_D s^2 + K_c K_p \tau_I s + K_c K_p = 0$$

Group by powers of $s$:
$$(\tau_I \tau_p + K_c K_p \tau_I \tau_D)s^2 + \tau_I(1 + K_c K_p)s + K_c K_p = 0$$

### Step 3 — Identify parameters
$$\omega_n^2 = \frac{K_c K_p}{\tau_I \tau_p + K_c K_p \tau_I \tau_D}, \qquad 2\zeta\omega_n = \frac{\tau_I(1 + K_c K_p)}{\tau_I \tau_p + K_c K_p \tau_I \tau_D}$$

### Step 4 — Effect of derivative
- $\tau_D$ **increases denominator of $\omega_n^2$** → $\omega_n$ **decreases** → slower response.
- $\tau_D$ also **increases damping** $\zeta$ → less overshoot.
- The derivative effectively "anticipates" the dead time, improving stability of FOPDT processes.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: P vs PI vs PID

| Property | P | PI | PID |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Offset ($e_{ss}$, non-integrating)** | $\dfrac{1}{1 + K_c K_p} \neq 0$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| **Offset (level/integrating)** | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| **Rise time $t_r$** | Decreases with $K_c$ | Slightly larger | Fastest |
| **Overshoot $M_p$** | Small / none for 1st order | Moderate | Reduced by $\tau_D$ |
| **Settling time $t_s$** | Moderate | Can oscillate | Best (if tuned) |
| **Noise sensitivity** | Low | Low | High (D amplifies noise) |
| **Suitable for Flow** | Yes (fast) | Yes | Often unnecessary |
| **Suitable for Level** | Yes (integrating) | Yes | Yes |
| **Suitable for Thermal** | Poor (offset) | Good | **Best** (handles dead time) |
| **Suitable for Pressure** | Acceptable | **Recommended** | If dead time exists |

---

## 3.6 Ziegler–Nichols Tuning for FOPDT (Open-Loop Method)

Given a step-test yielding $K_p$, $\tau_p$, $L$:

| Controller | $K_c$ | $\tau_I$ | $\tau_D$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P** | $\dfrac{\tau_p}{K_p L}$ | — | — |
| **PI** | $\dfrac{0.9 \, \tau_p}{K_p L}$ | $3L$ | — |
| **PID** | $\dfrac{1.2 \, \tau_p}{K_p L}$ | $2L$ | $0.5L$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical Closed-Loop Block Diagram (All Controllers)

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the universal negative-feedback architecture used across Level, Flow, Thermal, and Pressure experiments in PCAEL507.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    spNode["Set Point SP"] --> sumNode(("Sum"))
    pvNode["Process Variable PV"] --> sumNode
    sumNode --> errNode["Error e(t) = SP - PV"]
    errNode --> ctrlBlock["CONTROLLER Gc(s): P / PI / PID"]
    ctrlBlock --> mvNode["Manipulated Variable MV"]
    mvNode --> fceNode["Final Control Element Valve or Heater"]
    fceNode --> procNode["PROCESS Gp(s): Level / Flow / Thermal / Pressure"]
    procNode --> sensNode["Sensor and Transmitter H(s)"]
    sensNode --> pvNode
    procNode --> outNode["PV Output to Recorder"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The summing junction (circle) subtracts PV from SP to produce error.
- The controller block represents $G_c(s)$ — students swap P, PI, or PID settings in the lab software.
- The process block $G_p(s)$ changes based on the experiment (Level, Flow, Thermal, Pressure).
- The feedback path returns the measured PV.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of the PCAEL507 Lab Station

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph benchA["Lab Bench Setup"]
        tankUnit["Level Tank with Level Transmitter"]
        flowUnit["Flow Loop with Orifice and Flow Transmitter"]
        thermUnit["Thermal Module with Heater and RTD"]
        pressUnit["Pressure Vessel with Pressure Transducer"]
    end

    subgraph benchB["Controller Hardware"]
        dcsA["DCS / PLC Controller Card"]
        pidA["PID Algorithm Module"]
        tuneA["Tuning Knobs Kc, tauI, tauD"]
    end

    subgraph benchC["HMI and DAQ"]
        hmiA["HMI Screen displaying SP and PV"]
        scadaA["SCADA / PC with Data Logger"]
    end

    tankUnit --> dcsA
    flowUnit --> dcsA
    thermUnit --> dcsA
    pressUnit --> dcsA
    dcsA --> pidA
    pidA --> tuneA
    pidA --> hmiA
    pidA --> scadaA
```

---

## 4.3 Step-Response Comparison Flowchart (Which Controller to Choose?)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Identify Process Type from Open-Loop Test"] --> qA{"Does the PV return to a new steady value by itself?"}
    qA -- "No, keeps drifting" --> levelProc["Level / Integrating Process"]
    qA -- "Yes, settles to new value" --> qB{"Is there a noticeable time delay L between MV change and PV response?"}
    qB -- "No, fast response" --> fastProc["Flow or Pressure Process"]
    qB -- "Yes, L is large" --> slowProc["Thermal or FOPDT Process"]

    levelProc --> rec1["P controller is sufficient for setpoint tracking"]
    fastProc --> rec2["PI controller recommended; offset-free and quick"]
    slowProc --> rec3["PID controller recommended; derivative compensates dead time"]

    rec1 --> noteA["Z-N or Cohen-Coon tuning may be applied"]
    rec2 --> noteA
    rec3 --> noteA
```

---

## 4.4 Typical Closed-Loop Step Response Sketch (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph curves["Time Response Curves for a Step Change in SP"]
        dir0["Time Axis t"]
        dir1["PV Axis"]
    end
    spA["SP step at t=0"] -.-> pvResp["PV Response"]
    pvResp --> ptA["Rise Time tr"]
    pvResp --> ptB["Overshoot Mp"]
    pvResp --> ptC["Settling Time ts (2% band)"]
    pvResp --> ptD["Steady State PV = SP (offset = 0)"]
```

**Annotation of response features:**
- **P-only (non-integrating):** PV rises, may overshoot slightly, then settles *below* SP → **offset visible**.
- **PI:** PV rises, oscillates, then settles exactly at SP after integral "winds up".
- **PID:** PV rises fastest, smallest overshoot, settles quickly at SP.

---

## 4.5 Experimental Procedure Flowchart (KTU Standard)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1["Step 1: Identify the process module Level or Flow or Thermal or Pressure"] --> step2["Step 2: Perform open-loop step test and record PV"]
    step2 --> step3["Step 3: From PV response, estimate Kp, taup, L"]
    step3 --> step4["Step 4: Apply Ziegler-Nichols or trial-error tuning"]
    step4 --> step5["Step 5: Close the loop with P controller; record tr, ts, Mp, ess"]
    step5 --> step6["Step 6: Switch to PI controller; retune; record same metrics"]
    step6 --> step7["Step 7: Switch to PID controller; retune; record metrics"]
    step7 --> step8["Step 8: Tabulate results; plot PV vs t for each controller"]
    step8 --> step9["Step 9: Compare responses and write conclusion"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the actual **PCAEL507 lab manual**, the same controller hardware is used for all four processes; only the process rig and sensor calibration change.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Define the terms: (a) Proportional Band, (b) Reset Time, (c) Rate Time, with their units.**

#### Model Answer:
- **(a) Proportional Band (PB):** The range of error (expressed as a percentage of span) over which the controller output changes from 0% to 100%. It is related to gain by $\text{PB}(\%) = \dfrac{100}{K_c} \times \text{Span}$. Unit: **%**.
- **(b) Reset Time ($\tau_I$):** The time required for the integral action alone to repeat the proportional action. Also called **Integral Time**. Unit: **minutes/repeat** or **seconds/repeat**.
- **(c) Rate Time ($\tau_D$):** The time by which the proportional action is advanced due to derivative action. Also called **Derivative Time** or **Pre-Act**. Unit: **minutes** or **seconds**.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**
**Why does a P controller on a first-order flow process exhibit a permanent offset, while a P controller on a level tank does not?**

#### Model Answer:
A P controller on a **first-order non-integrating process** (flow, pressure, thermal) has a finite DC gain $K_c K_p$. The closed-loop steady-state output is $y(\infty) = \dfrac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p} \neq 1$, leaving an offset of $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_c K_p}$.

In contrast, a **level tank is an integrating process** $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{s}$ with infinite DC gain at $s=0$. Even with P-only control, the closed-loop is $T(s) = \dfrac{K_c K_p}{s + K_c K_p}$ (a stable first-order system with unity DC gain), so $y(\infty) = 1$ and $e_{ss} = 0$. The integrator in the process itself provides the "reset" action.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`**

**(a)** Derive the closed-loop transfer function of a first-order process $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$ under (i) P controller, (ii) PI controller. Show clearly how the steady-state error differs in each case. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a thermal process modeled as $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p e^{-Ls}}{\tau_p s + 1}$ with $K_p = 2$, $\tau_p = 60$ s, $L = 5$ s, design a PID controller using the Ziegler–Nichols open-loop method. Compute the expected $K_c$, $\tau_I$, $\tau_D$. Also state the expected qualitative behavior (overshoot, settling time) compared to a PI controller. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 Marks

**Part (i): P controller**

$G_c(s) = K_c$. Open-loop: $G_c G_p = \dfrac{K_c K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$.

Closed-loop:
$$T(s) = \frac{K_c K_p / (\tau_p s + 1)}{1 + K_c K_p / (\tau_p s + 1)} = \frac{K_c K_p}{\tau_p s + 1 + K_c K_p} = \frac{K_c K_p / (1 + K_c K_p)}{\dfrac{\tau_p}{1 + K_c K_p}\, s + 1}$$

Steady-state response to unit step:
$$y(\infty) = T(0) = \frac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}$$

Steady-state error:
$$e_{ss} = 1 - y(\infty) = \frac{1}{1 + K_c K_p} \neq 0$$

So P-only yields **non-zero offset** for a first-order process. **(3 Marks)**

**Part (ii): PI controller**

$G_c(s) = \dfrac{K_c(\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s}$.

Open-loop:
$$L(s) = \frac{K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s(\tau_p s + 1)}$$

Closed-loop:
$$T(s) = \frac{K_c K_p (\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I \tau_p s^2 + \tau_I(1 + K_c K_p) s + K_c K_p}$$

This is a second-order system. Using Final Value Theorem with $R(s) = 1/s$:
$$y(\infty) = T(0) = \frac{K_c K_p (0 + 1)}{0 + 0 + K_c K_p} = 1$$

Therefore:
$$e_{ss} = 1 - 1 = 0$$

**Conclusion:** PI eliminates offset because the integrator in $G_c$ provides infinite DC gain. The price is possible oscillation, but $\tau_I$ can be tuned to balance speed and stability. **(4 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Apply Z-N formulas for FOPDT process:**
$$K_c = \frac{1.2 \, \tau_p}{K_p L}, \qquad \tau_I = 2L, \qquad \tau_D = 0.5L$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**
$$K_c = \frac{1.2 \times 60}{2 \times 5} = \frac{72}{10} = 7.2$$

$$\tau_I = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ s}$$

$$\tau_D = 0.5 \times 5 = 2.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Expected qualitative behavior:**
- **Compared to PI:** PID with $\tau_D = 2.5$ s will produce **smaller overshoot** and **faster settling time**, because the derivative action compensates for the 5-second dead time by "anticipating" the PV change.
- However, Z-N tuning is aggressive and typically yields **20–30% overshoot**; in practice, lab students must **detune** $K_c$ to ~50–60% of $7.2$.
- The derivative term **amplifies sensor noise**; in the lab, derivative action is often **filtered** or **disabled** for flow/pressure processes with clean signals. **(7 Marks total)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 2]`**

**(a)** With suitable diagrams, explain how a P, PI, and PID controller respond to a step change in set point on a **level tank** process. Why is the derivative action *less critical* for level than for a thermal process? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A flow process has $K_p = 1.2$, $\tau_p = 2$ s, $L \approx 0$ (negligible dead time). (i) Design a PI controller using Z-N open-loop formulas. (ii) If $K_c$ is doubled from the Z-N value, what qualitative changes will you observe in the closed-loop response? Justify using the closed-loop transfer function. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Level tank process:** $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{s}$ (integrating).

**P controller response:**
- Closed-loop $T(s) = \dfrac{K_c K_p}{s + K_c K_p}$ — a first-order lag with time constant $\tau_{cl} = \dfrac{1}{K_c K_p}$.
- Unit-step response: $y(t) = 1 - e^{-K_c K_p t}$.
- PV smoothly approaches SP **without offset** because the process itself integrates.
- Larger $K_c$ → faster rise, but very high $K_c$ may saturate the valve. **(2 Marks)**

**PI controller response:**
- Adds an integrator in the controller, making the open-loop $L(s) = \dfrac{K_c K_p(\tau_I s + 1)}{\tau_I s^2}$ (a Type-2 system).
- Step response: faster initial rise, slight overshoot, then settles at SP with no offset.
- Since the level process already has integral behavior, the controller's integral mainly helps reject **disturbances** (e.g., outflow changes) more aggressively. **(2 Marks)**

**PID controller response:**
- Adds derivative term; response is even faster, with reduced overshoot.
- For level processes, the derivative provides only marginal benefit because there is **little dead time** to compensate. **(1 Mark)**

**Why derivative is less critical for level than for thermal:**
- Thermal processes have a noticeable transport delay $L$ (e.g., 5–30 s) between heater input and temperature sensor reading. The derivative term can *predict* future error, partially cancelling this delay.
- Level processes have **negligible dead time** (the level sensor reads almost immediately), so derivative action offers little additional benefit and mainly amplifies sensor noise. **(2 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Z-N PI tuning for FOPDT with $L \approx 0$:**
The classical Z-N open-loop formula requires $L > 0$. For negligible dead time, we use the **Cohen-Coon** or simply tune by setting a reasonable $\tau_I$:

A practical approach for flow with small $L$:
- Choose $K_c$ based on desired bandwidth: $K_c \approx \dfrac{\tau_p}{K_p \cdot L_{eff}}$ where $L_{eff} \approx 0.1\tau_p$.
- $L_{eff} = 0.1 \times 2 = 0.2$ s, $K_p = 1.2$.
- $K_c = \dfrac{0.9 \times 2}{1.2 \times 0.2} = \dfrac{1.8}{0.24} = 7.5$.
- $\tau_I = 3 L_{eff} = 0.6$ s. **(3 Marks)**

**Step 2 — Closed-loop TF with $K_c = 7.5$:**
$$\tau_{cl} = \frac{\tau_p}{1 + K_c K_p} = \frac{2}{1 + 7.5 \times 1.2} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.2 \text{ s}$$

$K_{cl} = \dfrac{7.5 \times 1.2}{10} = 0.9$ (offset $\approx 0.1$, i.e., 10%).

**Step 3 — If $K_c$ is doubled to $15$:**
- New $\tau_{cl} = \dfrac{2}{1 + 15 \times 1.2} = \dfrac{2}{19} = 0.105$ s — **faster response**.
- New $K_{cl} = \dfrac{18}{19} \approx 0.947$ — **offset reduces to ~5.3%**.
- However, the open-loop gain has jumped from $9$ to $18$, **shrinking stability margins**.
- In the lab, students will likely observe **faster rise** but **larger overshoot and possible oscillation** if $\tau_I$ is not adjusted. **(4 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PCAEL507 Module 2 Examinations:**
> 1. **Forgetting to compute $e_{ss}$ explicitly** — Examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for the steady-state error calculation. Students often jump to "no offset" without showing $T(0)$.
> 2. **Confusing "Reset Time" with "Derivative Time"** — $\tau_I$ (reset) removes offset; $\tau_D$ (rate) anticipates. Mixing them up is a frequent error.
> 3. **Wrong Z-N formula for PI** — Some students use $K_c = 1.2 \tau_p / (K_p L)$ (PID formula) when the question asks for PI. Always check: PI uses $0.9$, PID uses $1.2$.
> 4. **Ignoring dead time in thermal process** — Thermal FOPDT model has $e^{-Ls}$. Skipping $L$ in analysis loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Claiming P-controller has no offset on level** — true, but must be **justified** by writing $G_p(s) = K_p/s$ and showing $T(0) = 1$. Vague answers get partial credit only.
> 6. **Not labelling axes/diagrams** — In step-response sketches, always mark $t_r$, $t_s$, $M_p$, and $e_{ss}$ explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize these for guaranteed marks in PCAEL507 viva and written exams:**

- **P controller** = $K_c$ only. **Always produces offset** on non-integrating processes.
- **PI controller** = $K_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)$. **Eliminates offset** for step inputs. Default choice for **flow and pressure**.
- **PID controller** = $K_c\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s\right)$. **Best for thermal** and FOPDT processes with dead time.
- **Level process** is integrating ($G_p = K_p/s$); P-only suffices for setpoint tracking.
- **Flow process** is fast first-order; **PI** is the workhorse controller.
- **Thermal process** is FOPDT ($G_p = K_p e^{-Ls} / (\tau_p s + 1)$); **PID** is recommended.
- **Pressure process** is first-order; **PI** typically adequate.
- **Offset formula (P on 1st order):** $e_{ss} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_c K_p}$.
- **Closed-loop time constant (P on 1st order):** $\tau_{cl} = \dfrac{\tau_p}{1 + K_c K_p}$.
- **Closed-loop gain (P on 1st order):** $K_{cl} = \dfrac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p}$.
- **Z-N open-loop PI:** $K_c = 0.9\tau_p / (K_p L)$, $\tau_I = 3L$.
- **Z-N open-loop PID:** $K_c = 1.2\tau_p / (K_p L)$, $\tau_I = 2L$, $\tau_D = 0.5L$.
- **Derivative term** amplifies noise; filter or disable for noisy flow signals.
- **Performance index:** $\text{IAE} = \int_0^\infty \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$ — minimize for best tuning.
- **2nd-order parameters:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_c K_p / (\tau_I \tau_p)}$, $\zeta = (1 + K_c K_p) / (2 \sqrt{K_c K_p \tau_I / \tau_p})$.
- **% Overshoot:** $M_p = e^{-\zeta \pi / \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} \times 100\%$.
- **Settling time (2%):** $t_s \approx 4 / (\zeta \omega_n)$.
- **Lab procedure** = open-loop test → estimate $K_p, \tau_p, L$ → tune → close loop → record $t_r, t_s, M_p, e_{ss}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of responses of ON-OFF controller and ON-OFF controller with dead zone
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL507 - PROCESS CONTROL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of responses of ON-OFF controller and ON-OFF controller with dead zone

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — ON-OFF Controller

An **ON-OFF controller** is the simplest and most economical form of a feedback controller in which the manipulated variable (MV) can assume only two discrete states:

$$u(t) = \begin{cases} U_{\max} & \text{if } e(t) > 0 \\ U_{\min} & \text{if } e(t) < 0 \end{cases}$$

where the error signal is
$$e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$$

with $r(t)$ being the setpoint (SP) and $y(t)$ the process variable (PV). In a typical process, $U_{\max}$ is often **100 %** (full power / valve fully open) and $U_{\min}$ is **0 %** (no power / valve fully closed).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The ON-OFF controller is essentially a **relay-based discontinuous controller**. The output is binary, hence the name "two-position" or "bang-bang" controller used in KTU lab manuals.

### 1.2 Formal Definition — ON-OFF Controller with Dead Zone

A **dead zone** (also called *neutral zone* or *hysteresis band*) is a small range of error values around the setpoint within which the controller does **not** change its output. The controller only switches when the error leaves this band:

$$u(t) = \begin{cases} U_{\max} & \text{if } y(t) < r(t) - \delta \\ U_{\min} & \text{if } y(t) > r(t) + \delta \\ u(t^{-}) & \text{if } \vert e(t) \vert \leq \delta \end{cases}$$

Here, $\delta$ is the **dead zone half-width** and $u(t^{-})$ denotes the *last* output value (memory element).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Home Water Heater

Imagine your geyser at home set to **60 °C**:

* **Without dead zone (pure ON-OFF):** The instant water temperature dips to **59.9 °C**, the heater switches ON with full power; the moment it hits **60.1 °C**, it switches OFF completely. This causes the relay inside to *chatter* (click-click-click) and rapidly wear out.
* **With dead zone ($\delta = 1\,°\text{C}$):** The heater does **not** turn on until water cools to **59 °C**, and does not turn off until water reaches **61 °C**. The relay clicks far less frequently, and the heater lasts much longer. This is the engineering trade-off: a small error for a long equipment life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world mapping:** Industrial applications include household refrigerators, air conditioners (thermostats), soldering-iron temperature controls, and level switches in tanks — all of which are ON-OFF controllers in essence.

### 1.4 Why Study These in Process Control Lab?

| Aspect | Without Dead Zone | With Dead Zone |
|---|---|---|
| Relay life | **Short** (chattering) | **Long** |
| Steady-state error | Zero mean, high amplitude | Zero mean, smaller amplitude |
| Cycle period | Small, high frequency | Larger, controlled frequency |
| Practical use | Rare in industry | Common in process plants |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Modelling of the Process

Consider a typical first-order plus dead-time (FOPDT) process used in the lab (e.g., a temperature loop with heater + sensor):

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K \, e^{-L s}}{\tau s + 1}$$

The time-domain differential equation governing the process is:

$$\tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot u(t)$$

where the symbols have the meaning summarised in the cheat sheet below.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Compute the error:** $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$. The setpoint $r(t)$ is held constant during the experiment.
2. **Compare error with controller rules:**
   * If $e(t) > 0$ → process is below setpoint → output is forced to $U_{\max}$ (heater full ON).
   * If $e(t) < 0$ → process is above setpoint → output is forced to $U_{\min}$ (heater OFF).
3. **Process integrates the input:** Because $u(t)$ is a square wave (alternating between two levels), $y(t)$ becomes a *triangular/saw-tooth shaped oscillation* around the setpoint.
4. **Switching event:** The transition of $u(t)$ happens precisely when $y(t)$ crosses the setpoint (or, with dead zone, the upper/lower threshold).
5. **Sustained oscillation:** Since the plant has no integrator, the average value of $y(t)$ equals the setpoint — **no steady-state offset**, but the PV *oscillates* indefinitely.

### 2.3 Response of a First-Order Process to a Step Input

When the controller output changes from $U_{\min}$ to $U_{\max}$ at $t = t_0$, the process variable evolves as:

$$y(t) = K U_{\max} - \bigl(K U_{\max} - y(t_0)\bigr) e^{-(t-t_0)/\tau}$$

Similarly, when the controller switches back to $U_{\min}$:

$$y(t) = K U_{\min} - \bigl(K U_{\min} - y(t_0)\bigr) e^{-(t-t_0)/\tau}$$

The time required for $y(t)$ to move from $y_a$ to $y_b$ under a constant input $U$ is:

$$\Delta t = \tau \, \ln \left( \frac{K U - y_a}{K U - y_b} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition:** The exponential terms in the above equations are what gives the PV curve its **rounded corners** — a signature of a first-order plant.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Error signal | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Same units as PV |
| 2 | Pure ON-OFF output | $u = U_{\max}$ if $e > 0$; $u = U_{\min}$ if $e < 0$ | Binary output |
| 3 | ON-OFF with dead-zone output | $u = U_{\max}$ if $y < r - \delta$; $u = U_{\min}$ if $y > r + \delta$; else *hold* | $\delta > 0$ |
| 4 | First-order step response | $y(t) = K U (1 - e^{-t/\tau}) + y_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ | $y_0$ = initial value |
| 5 | Time to traverse $y_a \to y_b$ | $\Delta t = \tau \ln\!\bigl((K U - y_a)/(K U - y_b)\bigr)$ | under constant $U$ |
| 6 | Cycle period (no dead zone) | $T \approx 2 \tau \ln\!\bigl(\dfrac{K U_{\max} - K U_{\min}}{2K U_{\max} - r \cdot 2}\bigr)$ *(see derivation)* | seconds |
| 7 | Cycle period (with dead zone $\delta$) | $T_{\delta} \approx T + 2\tau \ln\!\bigl(\dfrac{K U_{\max} - r + \delta}{K U_{\max} - r - \delta}\bigr)$ | seconds |
| 8 | Amplitude of oscillation (no DZ) | $\to 0$ (theoretical); in practice, infinitesimally small | — |
| 9 | Amplitude of oscillation (with DZ) | $A = 2\delta$ | Engineering units |
| 10 | Mean value of PV | $\overline{y} = r$ (setpoint) | No offset |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Note:** When solving the table problems, **never** use the vertical bar `\|x\|` in raw text inside markdown cells — use `$\vert x \vert$` instead, otherwise the table breaks.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Domestic appliances:** Refrigerators, ACs, geysers, irons, room heaters.
* **Industrial process:** Level switches in tanks, pressure safety valves, low/high cut-off trips in pipelines.
* **Protection systems:** Over-temperature trip in transformers; over-pressure trips in compressors — all are ON-OFF controllers with a built-in dead zone (called a *hysteresis gap*) to avoid chattering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Period of Oscillation of Pure ON-OFF Controller

We assume a first-order plant with $U_{\min} = 0$ and $U_{\max} = U_M$ for clarity. Initially, let $y(0) = y_0$ with $y_0 < r$ (process below setpoint), so the controller drives $u = U_M$.

**Step 1: Time taken to reach setpoint from below ($t_1$):**

Using the formula derived earlier,

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= \tau \ln\!\left( \frac{K U_M - y_0}{K U_M - r} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Symmetry argument:**

Because the controller output is *symmetric* (alternates between $0$ and $U_M$) and the plant is linear and time-invariant, the time taken to fall from $r$ back down to $y_0$ equals $t_1$. So the **half-period** is $t_1$.

**Step 3: Total period:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{ON-OFF}} &= 2 \, t_1 \\
&= 2\tau \ln\!\left( \frac{K U_M - y_0}{K U_M - r} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: For a sustained oscillation, $y_0$ equals the lowest valley.**

By symmetry of the rising and falling exponentials, the lowest valley $y_v$ satisfies $K U_M - y_v = r - y_v$, which gives $y_v = r - (K U_M - r)$. Substituting back:

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{ON-OFF}} = 2\tau \ln\!\left( \frac{K U_M - r + (K U_M - r)}{K U_M - r} \right) = 2\tau \ln(2) \approx 1.386\,\tau}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **classic KTU result** — the natural period of an ON-OFF loop on a first-order plant is **$2\tau \ln 2$**, independent of $K$ and $r$! This is why KTU questions often test it.

### 3.2 Derivation: Period of Oscillation with Dead Zone

With dead zone $\delta$, the controller output is $U_M$ while $y < r - \delta$, and is $0$ while $y > r + \delta$. Hence the PV oscillates between $y_{\min} = r - \delta$ and $y_{\max} = r + \delta$.

**Step 1: Rising half-period (heater ON, $u = U_M$, PV moves from $r-\delta$ to $r+\delta$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t_{\text{rise}} &= \tau \ln\!\left( \frac{K U_M - (r - \delta)}{K U_M - (r + \delta)} \right) \\
&= \tau \ln\!\left( \frac{K U_M - r + \delta}{K U_M - r - \delta} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Falling half-period (heater OFF, $u = 0$, PV moves from $r+\delta$ to $r-\delta$):**

The process equation becomes $\tau \dot y + y = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y(t) &= (r + \delta)\, e^{-(t-t_2)/\tau}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for the time when $y = r - \delta$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta t_{\text{fall}} &= \tau \ln\!\left( \frac{r + \delta}{r - \delta} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total period with dead zone:**

$$
\boxed{T_{\delta} = \tau \ln\!\left( \frac{K U_M - r + \delta}{K U_M - r - \delta} \right) + \tau \ln\!\left( \frac{r + \delta}{r - \delta} \right)}
$$

**Step 4: Asymptotic observation (large $K U_M$):**

If $K U_M \gg r$, the second term dominates and

$$
T_{\delta} \approx \tau \ln\!\left( \frac{r + \delta}{r - \delta} \right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Increasing $\delta$ *increases* the period $T_{\delta}$ — this directly explains why dead zone is used to **reduce relay wear** (longer period = fewer switching events per hour).

### 3.3 Derivation: Mean Value (Steady-State Offset)

The mean of the oscillation over one full period is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{y} &= \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} y(t)\, dt
\end{aligned}
$$

Because the controller output has equal time spent at $U_M$ and at $0$ (or at $U_{\min}$), the *time-weighted average* input is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{u} &= \frac{U_M \cdot \Delta t_{\text{rise}} + U_{\min} \cdot \Delta t_{\text{fall}}}{T}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the steady-state first-order plant, $\overline{y} = K \overline{u}$, and we can show that $\overline{y} = r$ — i.e., **no offset**. This is a fundamental property distinguishing ON-OFF control from P-only control (which has offset).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick Question Watch:** Students often wrongly claim ON-OFF controllers have offset. They do **not**, because the controller output averages exactly what is needed to maintain the setpoint.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameters

| Parameter | Effect on Period $T$ | Effect on Amplitude $A$ | Effect on Relay Life |
|---|---|---|---|
| Increase $\tau$ | $T$ increases linearly | $A$ unchanged | $T$ increases → life longer |
| Increase $K$ | $T$ decreases (asymptotic) | $A$ unchanged | More frequent switching |
| Increase $\delta$ | $T_{\delta}$ increases | $A = 2\delta$ increases | Significantly longer life |
| Increase $U_{\max} - U_{\min}$ | $T$ decreases | $A$ unchanged | More aggressive cycling |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Closed-Loop Block Diagram of ON-OFF Control

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Setpoint r] --> B[Summing Junction]
    C[Process Variable y] --> B
    B --> D[Error e]
    D --> E[Relay Block: ON-OFF Logic]
    E --> F[Actuator u in 0 or Umax]
    F --> G[First Order Process]
    G --> H[Sensor]
    H --> C
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The feedback loop reads the PV through a sensor, subtracts it from the setpoint at the summing junction, and feeds the error into the ON-OFF relay block, which commands the actuator.

### 4.2 ON-OFF Controller with Dead Zone — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Setpoint r] --> B[Threshold Generator]
    B --> C1[Upper Threshold r plus delta]
    B --> C2[Lower Threshold r minus delta]
    D[Process Variable y] --> E[Comparator Bank]
    C1 --> E
    C2 --> E
    E --> F{Decision Logic}
    F --> G1[Hold last output if y between thresholds]
    F --> G2[Output equals Umax if y below lower threshold]
    F --> G3[Output equals Umin if y above upper threshold]
    G1 --> H[Final Control Output u]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The threshold generator creates the two switching limits from the setpoint; the comparator bank checks the PV against these limits; the decision logic emits the correct relay state.

### 4.3 Hysteresis Loop — Switching State Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[State A: Output equals Umax] -->|y exceeds r plus delta| S2[State B: Output equals Umin]
    S2 -->|y falls below r minus delta| S1
    S1 -.->|y in dead zone| S1
    S2 -.->|y in dead zone| S2
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is a *two-state machine* with memory — it is the essence of hysteresis. Once in a state, the system only leaves when the PV exits the corresponding dead-zone boundary.

### 4.4 Expected Response Waveforms (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TimeAxis[Time Evolution of Signals]
        T0[t equals 0] --> P1[PV rises exponentially toward KUmax]
        P1 --> P2[PV crosses r: controller switches OFF]
        P2 --> P3[PV falls exponentially toward zero]
        P3 --> P4[PV crosses r again: controller switches ON]
        P4 --> P1
    end
    subgraph Observations[Key Observations]
        O1[PV oscillates with period T] --> O2[Mean of PV equals r]
        O2 --> O3[No steady state offset]
    end
    TimeAxis --> Observations
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This functional flow captures the dynamic cycle of an ON-OFF controlled first-order process: rise, switch, fall, switch, repeat. With dead zone added, the same flow occurs but with the *switching events delayed* until the PV exits the $\pm \delta$ band.

### 4.5 Apparatus Block Diagram (for Lab Record)

| Block | Component in Lab |
|---|---|
| Process | Heated tank / Thermal process module |
| Sensor | RTD / Thermocouple with transmitter |
| Controller | PID trainer kit in **ON-OFF** mode |
| Final Control Element | Solid-state relay (SSR) / contactor |
| Recorder | PC with data acquisition (DAQ) / strip-chart |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Define an ON-OFF controller. Why is it called a "two-position" controller? Mention one industrial application.**

**Model Answer:**
An ON-OFF controller is a feedback controller whose output can assume only **two discrete values**, $U_{\max}$ and $U_{\min}$, depending on the sign of the error $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$. It is called a *two-position* controller because the actuator can take only two positions (e.g., fully ON or fully OFF). Application: **domestic refrigerator thermostat**.

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the dead zone in an ON-OFF controller? How does it affect the period of oscillation?**

**Model Answer:**
A dead zone (or hysteresis band) is a small range $\pm\delta$ around the setpoint within which the controller **does not change** its output. Increasing $\delta$ **increases the period of oscillation** $T_{\delta}$ (as derived in Section 3.2), thereby reducing relay cycling and improving equipment life.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a closed-loop ON-OFF control system. Derive the expression for the period of oscillation of a first-order plant under ON-OFF control.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A temperature process has $K = 2\,°\text{C}/\%$, $\tau = 60\,\text{s}$, $U_{\max} = 100\,\%$, $U_{\min} = 0\,\%$, and setpoint $r = 80\,°\text{C}$. Calculate (i) the steady-state mean PV, (ii) the period of oscillation, and (iii) the time taken for the PV to first reach the setpoint from ambient $25\,°\text{C}$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Operation and Derivation:**
The block diagram is given in Section 4.1. The output of the controller is

$$u(t) = \begin{cases} U_{\max} & \text{if } e(t) > 0 \\ U_{\min} & \text{if } e(t) < 0 \end{cases}$$

For a first-order plant $\tau \dot y + y = K u$, the half-period during heating is

$$t_1 = \tau \ln\!\left( \frac{K U_{\max} - y_0}{K U_{\max} - r} \right)$$

By symmetry, the full period is $T = 2 t_1$. For a sustained oscillation around $r$, the valley $y_v$ is symmetric, leading to:

$$\boxed{T = 2\tau \ln 2 \approx 1.386\,\tau}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

(i) **Steady-state mean PV:** From Section 3.3, $\overline{y} = r = \mathbf{80\,°C}$ (no offset).

(ii) **Period of oscillation:** $T = 2 \tau \ln 2 = 2 \times 60 \times 0.693 = \mathbf{83.18\,s}$.

(iii) **Time to first reach setpoint from 25 °C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= \tau \ln\!\left( \frac{K U_{\max} - 25}{K U_{\max} - 80} \right) \\
&= 60 \ln\!\left( \frac{2 \times 100 - 25}{2 \times 100 - 80} \right) \\
&= 60 \ln\!\left( \frac{175}{120} \right) \\
&= 60 \times \ln(1.4583) \\
&= 60 \times 0.3773 = \mathbf{22.64\,s}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of a dead zone (hysteresis band) in an ON-OFF controller. Derive the expression for the period of oscillation when a dead zone of half-width $\delta$ is introduced.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A level process has $K = 0.5$, $\tau = 40\,\text{s}$, $U_{\max} = 100\,\%$, $U_{\min} = 0\,\%$, and setpoint $r = 30$ units. A dead zone of $\delta = 2$ units is introduced. Calculate (i) the amplitude of oscillation, (ii) the new period of oscillation, and (iii) comment on relay life compared to a pure ON-OFF controller.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:** As shown in Section 3.2,

$$T_{\delta} = \tau \ln\!\left( \frac{K U_{\max} - r + \delta}{K U_{\max} - r - \delta} \right) + \tau \ln\!\left( \frac{r + \delta}{r - \delta} \right)$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

(i) **Amplitude:** $A = 2\delta = \mathbf{4}$ units (peak-to-peak).

(ii) **Period of oscillation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\delta} &= 40 \ln\!\left( \frac{0.5 \times 100 - 30 + 2}{0.5 \times 100 - 30 - 2} \right) + 40 \ln\!\left( \frac{30 + 2}{30 - 2} \right) \\
&= 40 \ln\!\left( \frac{22}{18} \right) + 40 \ln\!\left( \frac{32}{28} \right) \\
&= 40 \ln(1.2222) + 40 \ln(1.1429) \\
&= 40 \times 0.2007 + 40 \times 0.1335 \\
&= 8.028 + 5.340 = \mathbf{13.37\,s}
\end{aligned}
$$

(iii) **Comment on relay life:** The period with dead zone ($13.37\,\text{s}$) is *longer* than the period of a pure ON-OFF controller ($T = 2 \tau \ln 2 = 55.45 \times \ln 2 \approx$ — *with $K U_{\max} = 50 \gg r = 30$, the pure ON-OFF period is approximately $\tau \ln[(K U_{\max} - r)/r] \approx 40 \ln(20/30) \approx$ — *qualitatively*, the dead zone increases the cycle time, meaning **fewer relay switching events per hour**, thereby **prolonging relay life** substantially.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. Forgetting that the *amplitude of oscillation equals $2\delta$* — many students write it as just $\delta$. That costs 2 marks easily.
> 2. Mixing up the two natural log terms in the period formula; the first term corresponds to *heating* and the second to *cooling*. Skipping the second term is a 3-mark loss.
> 3. Stating that the ON-OFF controller has steady-state offset — it does **not**. Writing otherwise invites 0 marks for the conceptual part.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ON-OFF controller** is a **two-position (bang-bang)** controller; its output is either $U_{\max}$ or $U_{\min}$.
- **Error definition:** $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ — always setpoint minus process variable.
- **Pure ON-OFF period** on a first-order plant: $T = 2\tau \ln 2 \approx 1.386\,\tau$ (independent of $K$ and $r$).
- **Dead zone $\pm \delta$** introduces a hysteresis band; the controller output *holds* its last value while $\vert e(t) \vert \leq \delta$.
- **Amplitude of oscillation with dead zone:** $A = 2\delta$.
- **Mean PV equals the setpoint** — there is **no steady-state offset**.
- **Larger dead zone** → longer period → fewer relay operations → longer relay life → larger amplitude trade-off.
- **Common industrial examples:** Refrigerator, geyser, AC, soldering iron, pressure cut-off switches.
- **Lab precautions:** Always start the data logger before energising the heater; ensure the RTD/thermocouple is fully immersed; record at least 4–5 complete cycles to compute the period accurately.
- **Graph plotting tip:** When drawing the response, mark the *switching points* (where $u(t)$ flips) and the *peak/valley points* (where $y(t)$ reverses direction). This is what KTU examiners look for in the lab record.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Controller tuning using continuous cycling method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAEL507 - PROCESS CONTROL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Controller tuning using continuous cycling method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Controller tuning** is the procedure of adjusting the controller parameters (Proportional gain $K_c$, Integral time $T_i$, Derivative time $T_d$) so that the closed-loop control system satisfies the desired performance specifications such as stability, minimum overshoot, zero steady-state error, and acceptable transient response.

The **Continuous Cycling Method** (also known as the **Ziegler–Nichols Closed-Loop Tuning Method** or **Ultimate Gain Method**) is an *in-situ* experimental technique where the controller is placed in automatic (closed-loop) mode with integral and derivative actions disabled, and the proportional gain is gradually increased until the process exhibits **sustained oscillations** at a constant amplitude and period. The corresponding gain and period are called the **Ultimate Gain ($K_u$)** and **Ultimate Period ($P_u$)**, which form the basis for calculating the final controller settings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The continuous cycling method is a **closed-loop tuning technique** based on the **stability boundary** of the system. It is purely experimental and does not require a process model (unlike the reaction curve method).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cruise Control" Analogy

Imagine you are driving a car and trying to maintain a constant speed of **60 km/h** on a flat road. You press the accelerator lightly. The car accelerates but overshoots — you tap the brake. It slows down too much — you press the accelerator harder. The car oscillates around 60 km/h.

Now, suppose you keep increasing the accelerator sensitivity (proportional gain) until your car begins to oscillate continuously between **62 km/h and 58 km/h** without ever settling. That "sensitivity" is the **Ultimate Gain ($K_u$)**, and the time between two consecutive peaks is the **Ultimate Period ($P_u$)**.

> In this experiment, you deliberately push the controller to the **edge of instability**, observe the resulting oscillation, and then dial it back to a safe, well-damped operating point. The amount you "dial back" is defined by the **Ziegler–Nichols tuning correlations**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

- **Ultimate Gain ($K_u$)**: The critical value of the proportional gain ($K_c$) at which the closed-loop response produces **sustained oscillations** of constant amplitude.
- **Ultimate Period ($P_u$)**: The time period of the sustained oscillations (in minutes), measured as the time between two successive peaks.
- **Quarter Amplitude Decay (QAD)**: A decay ratio of **1/4**, i.e., each successive peak is one-fourth the height of the previous peak. Ziegler–Nichols tuning produces a response that decays at this rate.
- **Closed-loop tuning**: Tuning done with the controller actively in automatic mode, regulating the process variable against the setpoint.
- **Neutral stability / Marginal stability**: The condition at $K_c = K_u$ where poles of the closed-loop transfer function lie on the imaginary axis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
> - Time is conventionally expressed in **minutes**.
> - $K_u$ is **dimensionless** (or has units reciprocal to the process gain for first-order systems).
> - The **decay ratio (DR)** for Ziegler–Nichols tuning is **0.25** (1/4).
> - The **offset** in P-only control after load disturbance is approximately **0.50** (i.e., 50% of the open-loop error).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

The continuous cycling method is rooted in the **classical control theory** of linear systems. The key insight is the **Routh–Hurwitz stability criterion**: as the proportional gain $K_c$ increases, the closed-loop poles of the system migrate from the left-half plane (stable) towards the right-half plane (unstable). At one specific value, they lie **exactly on the imaginary axis** — this is the stability boundary.

At this boundary, the system's response to a disturbance is a **pure sinusoid** with frequency $\omega_u$ (the **ultimate frequency**), and the corresponding period is:

$$P_u = \frac{2\pi}{\omega_u}$$

The conditions for sustained oscillation are:

$$\vert G_c(j\omega_u) \cdot G_p(j\omega_u) \cdot G_v(j\omega_u) \vert = 1 \quad \text{(magnitude condition)}$$

$$\angle G_c(j\omega_u) \cdot G_p(j\omega_u) \cdot G_v(j\omega_u) = -180° \quad \text{(phase condition)}$$

For a proportional-only controller, $G_c(s) = K_c$, so the magnitude condition reduces to $K_c = K_u = 1/\vert G_p(j\omega_u)G_v(j\omega_u)\vert$.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Experimental Logic

1. **Bring the process to a steady state** at the desired operating point. Ensure the setpoint is held constant.
2. **Set the controller to MANUAL mode** initially. Record the manipulated variable (MV) and the controlled variable (PV) values.
3. **Switch to AUTOMATIC mode** with **P-only control** (set $T_i = \infty$, $T_d = 0$).
4. **Start with a small value of $K_c$** (say 0.5 or 1.0) and introduce a small **setpoint change** or allow the system to stabilize.
5. **Gradually increase $K_c$** in small increments. After each increment, observe the nature of the response:
   - If response is **overdamped or underdamped (decaying)** → continue increasing $K_c$.
   - If response is **sustained with constant amplitude** → you have reached the **ultimate gain $K_u$**.
6. **Record $K_u$** — the gain value at which sustained oscillations are observed.
7. **Measure $P_u$** — the time between two consecutive peaks (or troughs) of the sustained oscillation.
8. **Reduce $K_c$ to a safe value** to bring the process back to a stable operating condition.
9. **Apply the Ziegler–Nichols correlations** to compute the final controller settings based on $K_u$ and $P_u$.

### 2.3 Ziegler–Nichols Tuning Correlations (The Heart of the Method)

| **Controller Type** | **Proportional Gain $K_c$** | **Integral Time $T_i$** | **Derivative Time $T_d$** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P-only** | $0.50 \, K_u$ | — | — |
| **PI** | $0.45 \, K_u$ | $P_u / 1.2$ | — |
| **PID** | $0.60 \, K_u$ | $P_u / 2$ | $P_u / 8$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these three rows** — they are the most frequently asked numerical question in KTU university exams. Any error in the multiplication factor (0.45, 0.60, etc.) or the division (1.2, 2, 8) will cost full marks.

### 2.4 Alternate Form (Using $K_c$ and $T_i$ as given above)

For a **PID controller**, the settings can be equivalently written as:
- $K_c = 0.6 \, K_u$
- $T_i = 0.5 \, P_u$ (Note: $P_u/2 = 0.5 P_u$)
- $T_d = 0.125 \, P_u$ (Note: $P_u/8 = 0.125 P_u$)

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Definition** | **Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ultimate Gain | $K_u$ | Proportional gain at marginal stability | dimensionless |
| Ultimate Period | $P_u$ | Period of sustained oscillation | minutes (min) |
| Reset Time (Integral Time) | $T_i$ | Time for integral action to repeat P-action | minutes per repeat (min) |
| Rate Time (Derivative Time) | $T_d$ | Time for derivative action to anticipate P-action | minutes |
| Decay Ratio | DR | Ratio of successive peak amplitudes | dimensionless |
| Quarter Amplitude Decay | QAD | DR = 0.25 (1/4) | dimensionless |
| Closed-loop Offset | $e_{ss}$ | $e_{ss} = e_{open} / (1 + K_c K_p)$ | % of span |
| Ultimate Frequency | $\omega_u$ | $2\pi / P_u$ | rad/min |
| Quarter Decay Period | $P_{QD}$ | Time for amplitude to decay to 1/4 | min |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The continuous cycling method is used in industries where:
- A **process model is not available** (e.g., complex chemical reactors, distillation columns, pH control loops).
- The process is **non-linear** and theoretical models are unreliable.
- **Field engineers** need to tune controllers on-site quickly.
- It is heavily used in **refineries, petrochemical plants, HVAC systems, paper mills, and pharmaceutical batch processes**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: How the Ziegler–Nichols Correlations are Obtained

The Ziegler–Nichols tuning rules are **empirical correlations** developed experimentally by John G. Ziegler and Nathaniel B. Nichols in 1942. They are derived from the requirement that the closed-loop response to a step load disturbance exhibits a **quarter-amplitude decay** (decay ratio = 0.25).

The analytical justification uses the following approach:

**Step 1: Characterize the process at the stability boundary**

At the ultimate gain $K_u$ and ultimate period $P_u$, the closed-loop transfer function has poles on the imaginary axis at $s = \pm j\omega_u$, where $\omega_u = 2\pi / P_u$. The closed-loop system under P-control is marginally stable.

**Step 2: Approximate the process as a first-order plus time-delay (FOPTD) system near the stability boundary**

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p \, e^{-D s}}{\tau s + 1}$$

The closed-loop characteristic equation under P-control is:

$$1 + K_c G_p(s) = 0 \Rightarrow 1 + K_c \cdot \frac{K_p \, e^{-D s}}{\tau s + 1} = 0$$

**Step 3: Apply the phase condition at $\omega = \omega_u$**

The phase contribution of the FOPTD system is:

$$\angle G_p(j\omega_u) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega_u \tau) - D \omega_u$$

Setting the total phase shift equal to $-180°$:

$$-\tan^{-1}(\omega_u \tau) - D \omega_u = -180°$$

This transcendental equation yields $\omega_u$ (and hence $P_u$) as a function of $D/\tau$.

**Step 4: Compute $K_u$ from the magnitude condition**

$$K_u = \frac{1}{K_p \cdot \vert G_p(j\omega_u)/K_p \vert} = \frac{\sqrt{(\omega_u \tau)^2 + 1}}{K_p}$$

**Step 5: Empirically relate $K_u$ and $P_u$ to the desired controller settings**

By simulating many processes and tuning them for QAD response, Ziegler and Nichols found the empirical constants:

$$\begin{aligned}
K_c^{\text{PI}} &= 0.45 \, K_u \\
T_i^{\text{PI}} &= P_u / 1.2 \\
K_c^{\text{PID}} &= 0.60 \, K_u \\
T_i^{\text{PID}} &= P_u / 2 \\
T_d^{\text{PID}} &= P_u / 8
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:** In a temperature control loop, the continuous cycling method yielded an ultimate gain $K_u = 8.0$ and an ultimate period $P_u = 6.0$ minutes. Calculate the controller settings for (a) P-only, (b) PI, and (c) PID controllers. Also calculate the integral action in repeats per minute for the PI controller.

**Solution:**

#### Part (a): P-only Controller

Using the Ziegler–Nichols correlation:
$$K_c = 0.50 \, K_u$$

Substituting $K_u = 8.0$:
$$K_c = 0.50 \times 8.0 = 4.0$$

$$\boxed{K_c = 4.0}$$

The integral and derivative actions are disabled ($T_i = \infty$, $T_d = 0$).

#### Part (b): PI Controller

Using the correlations:
$$K_c = 0.45 \, K_u = 0.45 \times 8.0 = 3.6$$

$$T_i = \frac{P_u}{1.2} = \frac{6.0}{1.2} = 5.0 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{K_c = 3.6, \quad T_i = 5.0 \text{ min}}$$

The integral action in **repeats per minute** is:
$$\text{Repeats/min} = \frac{1}{T_i} = \frac{1}{5.0} = 0.2 \text{ repeats/min}$$

#### Part (c): PID Controller

Using the correlations:
$$K_c = 0.60 \, K_u = 0.60 \times 8.0 = 4.8$$

$$T_i = \frac{P_u}{2} = \frac{6.0}{2} = 3.0 \text{ min}$$

$$T_d = \frac{P_u}{8} = \frac{6.0}{8} = 0.75 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{K_c = 4.8, \quad T_i = 3.0 \text{ min}, \quad T_d = 0.75 \text{ min}}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Why Each Multiplication Factor?

| **Factor** | **Empirical Meaning** | **Why This Value?** |
|---|---|---|
| $0.50 \, K_u$ for P | Half the ultimate gain | Gives a damping factor of ~0.5 (decaying oscillation) |
| $0.45 \, K_u$ for PI | Slightly less than P-only | Integral action speeds up the response, so a slightly lower $K_c$ is needed to maintain the same QAD |
| $0.60 \, K_u$ for PID | Higher than PI | Derivative action adds phase lead, allowing higher gain without instability |
| $T_i = P_u / 1.2$ for PI | Integral time ~ 83% of $P_u$ | Ensures integral action is fast enough to eliminate offset but not destabilize |
| $T_i = P_u / 2$ for PID | Integral time = 50% of $P_u$ | With derivative action, faster integral is possible |
| $T_d = P_u / 8$ for PID | Derivative time = 12.5% of $P_u$ | Provides enough phase lead for stability margin |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Closed-Loop Control System Block Diagram (During Continuous Cycling)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint SP] --> SUM[Comparator Sum Node]
    SUM -->|Error e_t| CTRL[P-Only Controller Kc]
    CTRL -->|MV m_t| VALVE[Control Valve Gv]
    VALVE -->|Flow q| PROC[Process Gp]
    PROC -->|PV y_t| SENSOR[Sensor Hm]
    SENSOR -->|Measurement| SUM
    PROC -.->|Oscillates at| OSC[Sustained Oscillation Pu]
    CTRL -.->|Ultimate Gain| UK[Ku Value]
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Explanation:**

1. **Setpoint (SP):** The desired value of the controlled variable (e.g., 75°C). Held constant during the experiment.
2. **Comparator (Sum Node):** Computes the error $e(t) = SP - PV$.
3. **P-only Controller ($K_c$):** Computes the controller output $m(t) = K_c \cdot e(t) + m_s$ (where $m_s$ is the manual reset / bias).
4. **Control Valve ($G_v$):** The final control element that adjusts the flow of the manipulated variable.
5. **Process ($G_p$):** The plant (e.g., heat exchanger, reactor, tank) being controlled.
6. **Sensor ($H_m$):** Measures the controlled variable (e.g., RTD, thermocouple) and sends the signal back to the comparator.
7. **Oscillation Detection:** During continuous cycling, the output $y(t)$ shows sustained sinusoidal oscillations, from which $K_u$ and $P_u$ are measured.

### 4.2 Experimental Procedure Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Bring Process to Steady State] --> B[Switch to AUTO Mode with P-Only]
    B --> C[Set Initial Kc to a Small Value]
    C --> D[Apply Small Setpoint Change]
    D --> E{Response Type}
    E -->|Decaying Oscillation| F[Increase Kc Incrementally]
    E -->|Sustained Oscillation| G[Record Ku and Pu]
    E -->|No Oscillation - Overdamped| F
    F --> H[Wait for Process to Stabilize]
    H --> E
    G --> I[Reduce Kc to Safe Value]
    I --> J[Apply Ziegler-Nichols Correlations]
    J --> K[Calculate Final Kc, Ti, Td]
    K --> L[Set New Controller Parameters]
    L --> M[Test Response and Fine-Tune if Needed]
    M --> N[End: Tuning Complete]
```

### 4.3 Response Curves at Different Gains (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Kc Less Than Ku] -->|Overdamped or Decaying| B[Stable Response]
    C[Kc Equal to Ku] -->|Sustained Oscillation| D[Marginal Stability]
    E[Kc Greater Than Ku] -->|Growing Oscillation| F[Unstable Response]
    G[Kc = 0.45 Ku to 0.60 Ku] -->|Quarter Amplitude Decay| H[Tuned Response per ZN]
```

**Explanation of the Four Regimes:**

| **Regime** | **Controller Gain** | **Response Nature** | **Action** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low gain | $K_c \ll K_u$ | Sluggish, overdamped | Increase $K_c$ |
| Critical gain | $K_c = K_u$ | Sustained oscillation | **Record $K_u$, $P_u$** |
| High gain | $K_c > K_u$ | Growing oscillation (unstable) | Reduce $K_c$ immediately |
| Tuned | $K_c = 0.45 K_u$ to $0.60 K_u$ | QAD response | Operating point |

### 4.4 Typical Quarter-Amplitude Decay Response

```
y(t)
  |        Peak 1 (A)
  |       /\         
  |      /  \  Peak 2 (A/4)  Peak 3 (A/16)
  |     /    \    /\          /\
  |    /      \  /  \   /\   /  \   
  |   /        \/    \ /  \ /    \  
  |__/_________/______X____X______\______>  t
     |         |      |    |      
     |         |      |    |      
  (Setpoint line)
     |←-P_u-→ |
        T_i
```

The **first peak** has amplitude $A$, the **second peak** has amplitude $A/4$, the **third peak** has amplitude $A/16$, and so on — each successive peak is **one-quarter** the height of the previous peak. The period between two consecutive peaks is approximately equal to the **ultimate period $P_u$**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define ultimate gain ($K_u$) and ultimate period ($P_u$) in the context of controller tuning.**

**Model Answer:**

**Ultimate Gain ($K_u$):** Ultimate gain is defined as the value of the proportional gain ($K_c$) at which the closed-loop control system, with proportional-only control, exhibits **sustained oscillations of constant amplitude** when subjected to a small disturbance or setpoint change. At this gain, the system is **marginally stable** — the closed-loop poles lie exactly on the imaginary axis of the s-plane.

**Ultimate Period ($P_u$):** Ultimate period is the **time period** of the sustained oscillations observed when the controller gain is set to $K_u$. It is measured as the time interval between two **consecutive peaks** (or troughs) of the oscillation curve. Mathematically, $P_u = 2\pi / \omega_u$, where $\omega_u$ is the ultimate frequency in rad/min.

These two parameters form the basis for the **Ziegler–Nichols closed-loop tuning method**, from which the final controller settings ($K_c$, $T_i$, $T_d$) are calculated.

#### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Ziegler–Nichols tuning correlations for a PID controller.**

**Model Answer:**

The Ziegler–Nichols tuning correlations for a **PID controller** based on the continuous cycling method are:

$$K_c = 0.6 \, K_u$$

$$T_i = \frac{P_u}{2}$$

$$T_d = \frac{P_u}{8}$$

Where $K_u$ is the ultimate gain and $P_u$ is the ultimate period. The proportional-only controller uses $K_c = 0.5 K_u$, and the PI controller uses $K_c = 0.45 K_u$ and $T_i = P_u / 1.2$.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **continuous cycling method** of controller tuning. Explain the step-by-step procedure to be followed during the experiment and state the precautions to be observed.

**Model Solution:**

The continuous cycling method, also called the **Ziegler–Nichols closed-loop tuning method**, is an experimental procedure used to determine the optimal settings of P, PI, or PID controllers for a process whose mathematical model is unknown.

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Bring the process to a steady state** at the desired operating point. The setpoint should be held at a typical value, and the process variable (PV) should be stable with no oscillations.

2. **Switch the controller to AUTOMATIC mode** with **proportional-only control** (set $T_i = \infty$ or its maximum value, and $T_d = 0$).

3. **Set the initial proportional gain to a small value** (typically 0.5 or 1.0, or about 10% of an estimated $K_u$).

4. **Apply a small setpoint change** (typically 2–5% of the setpoint range) to disturb the process and observe the response.

5. **If the response is overdamped or decays quickly**, gradually increase $K_c$ in small increments (e.g., 10–20% at a time). Allow the process to stabilize after each increment.

6. **Observe the response carefully**: If the response shows decaying oscillations, continue to increase $K_c$. When sustained oscillations of **constant amplitude** are obtained, the corresponding gain is the **ultimate gain ($K_u$)**.

7. **Record $K_u$** and **measure the ultimate period $P_u$** as the time between two consecutive peaks of the sustained oscillation.

8. **Reduce $K_c$ to a safe value** (e.g., 0.3 $K_u$) to bring the process back to a stable state.

9. **Apply the Ziegler–Nichols correlations** to compute the final settings:
   - P-only: $K_c = 0.5 K_u$
   - PI: $K_c = 0.45 K_u$, $T_i = P_u / 1.2$
   - PID: $K_c = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = P_u / 2$, $T_d = P_u / 8$

10. **Set the new parameters** on the controller and test the closed-loop response to a small setpoint change. Fine-tune if necessary.

**Precautions:**

- The process must be operating **within safe limits** (temperature, pressure, flow) throughout the experiment.
- Use a **small setpoint change** to avoid large overshoots that may damage equipment.
- **Do not exceed safe $K_c$** values — once sustained oscillations are observed, stop increasing $K_c$ immediately.
- Ensure the process is **linear** in the operating region. Highly non-linear processes may not exhibit clean sustained oscillations.
- The control valve should not **saturate** during the experiment. If the MV hits 0% or 100%, the results will be invalid.
- The process should be at **steady state** before the experiment begins.
- For safety, always have an **emergency shutdown** procedure in place.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In the continuous cycling experiment on a level control loop, the **ultimate gain** was found to be $K_u = 5.0$ and the **ultimate period** was $P_u = 4.0$ minutes. Calculate the controller settings for a **PI controller** and a **PID controller** using the Ziegler–Nichols method. Also express the integral action in **repeats per minute** for the PI controller.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $K_u = 5.0$, $P_u = 4.0$ min

**(i) For PI Controller:**

Using Ziegler–Nichols correlations:
$$K_c = 0.45 \, K_u = 0.45 \times 5.0 = 2.25$$

$$T_i = \frac{P_u}{1.2} = \frac{4.0}{1.2} = 3.333 \text{ min}$$

Integral action in repeats per minute:
$$\frac{1}{T_i} = \frac{1}{3.333} = 0.30 \text{ repeats/min}$$

$$\boxed{K_c = 2.25, \quad T_i = 3.33 \text{ min} \quad (0.30 \text{ repeats/min})}$$

**(ii) For PID Controller:**

$$K_c = 0.60 \, K_u = 0.60 \times 5.0 = 3.0$$

$$T_i = \frac{P_u}{2} = \frac{4.0}{2} = 2.0 \text{ min}$$

$$T_d = \frac{P_u}{8} = \frac{4.0}{8} = 0.5 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{K_c = 3.0, \quad T_i = 2.0 \text{ min}, \quad T_d = 0.5 \text{ min}}$$

#### Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **theoretical basis** of the continuous cycling method. How are the Ziegler–Nichols tuning correlations derived? Discuss the **concept of decay ratio** in the context of controller tuning.

**Model Solution:**

**Theoretical Basis:**

The continuous cycling method is based on the concept of **marginal stability** in closed-loop control systems. When a process is placed under P-only control, increasing the proportional gain $K_c$ causes the closed-loop poles to migrate from the left-half plane (stable region) towards the right-half plane (unstable region) in the s-plane.

At a specific value of $K_c = K_u$, the dominant poles lie **exactly on the imaginary axis** at $s = \pm j\omega_u$. At this point, the system becomes **marginally stable**, and its response to any disturbance is a **sustained sinusoidal oscillation** of frequency $\omega_u$ and period $P_u = 2\pi/\omega_u$.

The conditions for marginal stability are:
- **Magnitude condition:** $\vert K_u \cdot G_p(j\omega_u) \cdot G_v(j\omega_u) \vert = 1$
- **Phase condition:** $\angle G_p(j\omega_u) \cdot G_v(j\omega_u) = -180°$

**Derivation of Ziegler–Nichols Correlations:**

Ziegler and Nichols (1942) performed extensive experiments on various processes and determined the empirical relationship between the ultimate parameters ($K_u$, $P_u$) and the controller settings that produce a **quarter-amplitude decay (QAD)** response. The QAD criterion means that each successive peak in the closed-loop response is one-fourth the amplitude of the previous peak, corresponding to a **decay ratio of 0.25**.

Through empirical fitting, they established:
$$K_c = \alpha \, K_u, \quad T_i = \beta \, P_u, \quad T_d = \gamma \, P_u$$

Where the constants $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$ are tuned for each controller type to achieve QAD:
- P-only: $\alpha = 0.50$
- PI: $\alpha = 0.45$, $\beta = 1/1.2 \approx 0.833$
- PID: $\alpha = 0.60$, $\beta = 0.50$, $\gamma = 0.125$

**Concept of Decay Ratio:**

The **decay ratio (DR)** is defined as the ratio of the amplitude of the second peak to that of the first peak in the closed-loop transient response:

$$DR = \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

- **Overdamped:** No oscillations, DR is undefined. Sluggish response.
- **Quarter-Amplitude Decay (QAD):** $DR = 0.25$. This is the Ziegler–Nichols target — a good compromise between speed and damping.
- **Underdamped:** $DR < 0.25$. More oscillations before settling.
- **Sustained oscillation:** $DR = 1$. Marginally stable (at $K_c = K_u$).
- **Unstable:** $DR > 1$. Growing oscillations.

A QAD response typically settles in about **3 to 5 oscillations** and is widely accepted as an industrially optimal trade-off between fast response and low overshoot.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A temperature control loop is to be tuned using the continuous cycling method. The following observations are recorded: when $K_c = 6.0$, the response shows decaying oscillations with period 3.5 min; when $K_c = 9.0$, the oscillations grow slowly. Estimate the **ultimate gain $K_u$** and **ultimate period $P_u$**, and recommend the **PID settings** using Ziegler–Nichols correlations.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Observations:**
- At $K_c = 6.0$: Decaying oscillations, period = 3.5 min (system is below the stability boundary)
- At $K_c = 9.0$: Growing oscillations (system is above the stability boundary)

**Step 1: Interpolate to find $K_u$**

Since $K_u$ lies between 6.0 and 9.0, and at $K_u = 6.0$ the response decays (stable) while at $K_c = 9.0$ the response grows (unstable), the ultimate gain is somewhere in between. A reasonable interpolation gives:

$$K_u \approx \frac{6.0 + 9.0}{2} = 7.5$$
